nofx.rawNotes:
NOFX formed in Los Angeles in 1983

 from 

guitarist Eric Melvin and bassist/vocalist Mike Burkett, known as Fat Mike

, with 

drummer Erik Sandin

. 

The name came from a Boston hardcore punk band called Negative FX

. 

Bassist/lead vocalist Fat Mike, rhythm guitarist Eric Melvin and drummer Erik Sandin were original founding and longest-serving members who appeared on every release

, though 

Sandin departed briefly in 1985, only to rejoin the following year

. 

El Hefe, whose real name is Aaron Abeyta, joined the band in 1991 to play lead guitar and trumpet

.



Punk was a raging scene during the 1980s in Southern California

, and NOFX emerged from this underground hardcore landscape. 

They frequented the Cathay de Grande club on Selma and Argyle in Hollywood, where on Tuesday nights new bands would play for one dollar admission, catching acts like D.R.I., Minor Threat, and Black Flag

. 

In 1994, during the 1990s punk rock revival, NOFX released their fifth studio album Punk in Drublic, which became one of their most successful albums, peaking at number twelve on Billboard's Heatseekers chart and obtaining gold status

. Other key albums include 

Liberal Animation recorded in 1988 with Brett Gurewitz of Bad Religion

, 

S&M Airlines through Epitaph in 1989

, 

White Trash, Two Heebs and a Bean, released in November 1992

, and 

So Long and Thanks for All the Shoes in 1997

. 

Early releases appeared on independent label Mystic Records, transitioning to Epitaph Records before the majority of the catalog shifted to Fat Wreck Chords starting in 1991

, Fat Mike's own label.



When their "Leave It Alone" single got airplay on active rock radio, Fat Mike stated they decided not to send the video to MTV because "We just didn't want to be a part of that machine, of that 'punk wave'"

. 

The Decline, an 18-minute single-track extended play, served as a fiery and cynical social commentary, released in 1999

. 

During a 2010 SXSW performance, it was revealed that audience members unknowingly drank Fat Mike's urine during the stunt, and he is now banned from the South by Southwest festival

.



NOFX used humor to cover issues like racism and American middle-class plight with reggae vocal twists, including tracks poking fun at lesbian relationships, and expressed disgust with commercial radio through sardonically titled songs

. 

The band became increasingly political in recent years with a leftist lean, as evidenced by songs like No Fun in Fundamentalism and Idiot Son of an Asshole

. 

The band retired in October 2024 after a farewell tour, but released the single "Minnesota Nazis" in January 2026 as a protest song

.

the ramones.rawNotes:
The Ramones were an American punk rock band formed in the New York City neighborhood Forest Hills, Queens in 1974.

 

The original members were Joey Ramone (Jeffrey Hyman), Johnny Ramone (John Cummings), Dee Dee Ramone (Douglas Colvin), and Tommy Ramone (Thomas Erdelyi).

 

All members adopted pseudonyms ending with the surname Ramone, although none were biologically related; they were inspired by Paul McCartney, who used the stage name Paul Ramon when the Beatles were still calling themselves The Silver Beetles.





Founded in New York City in 1974, the Ramones cultivated a simple three-chord sound that became the foundation of punk rock.

 

In ripped jeans and black leather jackets, the Ramones made their reputation with almost-nonstop touring and energetic live performances, notably at New York City's CBGB club.

 

Along with bands such as the New York Dolls, Television, Blondie, Patti Smith Group, and Talking Heads, The Ramones were a focal point of the collective punk scene at the club CBGB in New York City.





Influenced by the rebelliousness of their contemporaries the New York Dolls and by 1960s pop music (especially bubblegum and surf music), the Ramones brought their back-to-basics approach to such albums as their eponymous debut (1976) and Rocket to Russia (1977).

 

In January 1976 the band took a break from their live performances to prepare for recording at Plaza Sound studio. Sessions began later that month and were completed within a week for $6,400; the instruments took three days and the vocal parts were recorded in four days.

 

Produced by Tony Bongiovi and Tommy Ramone, and recorded at Sundragon Studios in New York, the Leave Home album solidified the Ramones' place in the punk scene with its slightly cleaner production compared to their debut.





Their tour of England in 1976 proved a major inspiration for the punk movement in Britain, where they enjoyed greater commercial success than at home.

 

On December 31, 1977, the Ramones recorded It's Alive, a live concert double album, at the Rainbow Theatre, London, which was released in April 1979.

 One remarkable anecdote: 

Johnny Ramone recalled, "We had the songs for the first three albums when we did the first one. We already had 30 to 35 songs, and we recorded them in the chronological order that we wrote them."

 Another notable moment came years later when 

Joey and Marky, who had been involved in a feud, buried the hatchet and made up on live radio on The Howard Stern Show

 in 1999. 

The song "Bonzo Goes to Bitburg" was written, primarily by Joey, in protest of Ronald Reagan's visit to a German military cemetery, which included graves of Waffen-SS soldiers.





The band's recording career began with their self-titled debut album, Ramones (1976, Sire Records), which featured 14 tracks clocking in at just 29 minutes and established their template of short, fast songs with simple chord progressions and themes of juvenile delinquency and pop culture.

 

The Ramones performed 2,263 concerts, touring virtually nonstop for 22 years, and released fourteen studio albums.

 

In 1996, after a tour as part of the Lollapalooza music festival, they played a farewell concert in Los Angeles and disbanded.

ramones.rawNotes:
The original Ramones members were Joey Ramone (Jeffrey Hyman), Johnny Ramone (John Cummings), Dee Dee Ramone (Douglas Colvin), and Tommy Ramone (Thomas Erdelyi)

. 

The band formed in Queens, New York, in 1974

, specifically 

in the Forest Hills neighborhood

. 

All members adopted pseudonyms ending with Ramone, inspired by Paul McCartney using the stage name Paul Ramon when the Beatles were still calling themselves The Silver Beetles

.



The Ramones helped establish the punk movement in the United States and are often recognized as one of the first bands of the genre

. 

They made their reputation with almost-nonstop touring and energetic live performances at New York City's CBGB club

, which 

came to be known as the birthplace of punk

. 

The Ramones performed 2,263 concerts over 22 years and released fourteen studio albums

.



Influenced by the rebelliousness of the New York Dolls and 1960s pop music, especially bubblegum and surf music

, the band produced landmark albums. 

Classic albums included Ramones (1976), Rocket to Russia (1977), and Road to Ruin (1978)

. 

The debut was recorded at Plaza Sound studio in January 1976 and completed within a week for $6,400

. 

Leave Home was done and mixed in various studios including Le Studio in Quebec, Canada, and Track Records in DC

. 

Rocket to Russia involved tracks recorded at MediaSound

.



The Ramones cultivated a simple three-chord sound played at a blistering tempo, frequently lasting little more than two minutes

. 

Their song "Pinhead" became one of the band's signature songs with its chanted refrain of "Gabba gabba hey!"

 

The single "Sheena Is a Punk Rocker" landed on the Billboard Hot 100

. Lyrically, 

their often willfully inane lyrics were so stupid they were smart, as seen in songs like "Blitzkrieg Bop" and "I Wanna Be Sedated"

. 

Joey cited Ronnie Spector as a favorite singer, and his love songs for the band were reminiscent of 1960s girl group sound

.



The band sold out for their first London performance with an audience of roughly 3,000

. Another pivotal moment involved a near-disaster: 

Drummer Clem Burke from Blondie, adopted as Elvis Ramone, was fired after two performances in August 1987 because his drumming could not keep up with the rest of the band

. 

In 1996, after a tour as part of Lollapalooza, they played a farewell concert in Los Angeles and disbanded

.

the descendents.rawNotes:
The Descendents are an American punk rock band formed in Manhattan Beach, California, in 1977, by guitarist Frank Navetta, bassist Tony Lombardo and drummer Bill Stevenson as a power pop/surf punk band.

 

In 1979, they enlisted Stevenson's school friend Milo Aukerman as a singer, and reappeared as a melodic hardcore punk band, becoming a major player in the hardcore scene developing in Los Angeles at the time.

 

Since 1986, the band's lineup has consisted of vocalist Milo Aukerman, drummer Bill Stevenson, guitarist Stephen Egerton, and bassist Karl Alvarez.





The lineup of Navetta, Lombardo, and Stevenson recorded the band's debut single at Media Art studios and released it on their own label, Orca Records, named after Stevenson's fishing boat.

 

Adding singer Milo Aukerman, they next released the Fat EP in 1981 and their debut album Milo Goes to College in 1982 through New Alliance.

 

For the recording of their debut album Milo Goes to College in June 1982, the band worked at Total Access Recording in Redondo Beach, California, with Spot, who had also engineered and produced the Fat EP.

 

In 1987 New Alliance was absorbed by SST Records, who reissued the Descendents' previous material and released their fourth album, All.

 

Some of their most notable albums include Milo Goes to College (1982), I Don't Want to Grow Up (1985), All (1987), Everything Sucks (1996), and Hypercaffium Spazzinate (2016).

 

In 2002, six years after cameo-ing on the Descendents' excellent 1996 comeback LP, Everything Sucks, Navetta and Lombardo reconvened with Stevenson at the Blasting Room, his studio in Fort Collins, Colorado.





Steven Blush, author of American Hardcore: A Tribal History, remarked that their "cheeky love songs disguised as hardcore blasts became the most aped formula in rock."

 

For many listeners, the Descendents story begins with Milo Goes to College, an album that reconciled the antisocial fury of the band's Southern California peers like Black Flag with diary-entry vulnerability and sing-along hooks.

 

Descendents have diverged from their punk peers, perhaps because of the Beach Boys influences, and sang about topics that touched many young punks' lives like parents, food, girls and being rejected.

 

The song ALL was actually written at the time of the philosophy's conception around 1980, in the midst of the song-writing fury that produced a number of the tunes from the "Fat" EP, including "I Like Food." Stevenson co-wrote these songs with best friend/fishing buddy/Descendents "Fifth Member," Pat McQuiston. The two came up with the concept while working a late night on the water, and fishing and coffee remained themes for Descendents songs and spiritual aids in the quest for ALL, which they define quite conventionally as "the total extent."





Their friend Stevenson — an eager fan of Nolte's other band, Sixties-influenced power-pop outfit the Last — joined on drums, and Navetta recruited Tony Lombardo, a bassist close to 20 years older than the other members who lived near Navetta's siblings' Long Beach home at 9th and Walnut, where the fledgling band held practices in the garage.

 

The Milo Goes to College lineup dissolved by 1983, following Aukerman's real-life departure for college. Navetta moved to Oregon, where his family lived; Lombardo parted ways with the Descendents after appearing on their second album, I Don't Want to Grow Up, and before a 1985 tour, feeling weighed down by the pressures of adult life.

 

But he says he still regrets leaving the band all those years ago, when he chose domestic stability — a fiancée, a job at the post office, and the actual "Suburban Home," in Lakewood, California, that he non-ironically yearned for on one of the best-known songs he wrote for

anti-flag.rawNotes:
Anti-Flag was an American punk rock band formed in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, in 1988.

 

For the majority of the band's career, their members included Justin Sane (vocals and guitar), Chris Head (guitar), Pat Thetic (drums), and Chris Barker, also known as Chris No. 2 (vocals and bass).

 The band's real core was 

Justin Geever, a.k.a. Justin Sane, co-founded in Pittsburgh in 1993 (after an earlier 1988 formation)

, alongside 

Pat Thetic, who founded the band

. 

The band was known for its left-wing political activism.





In 1996, Anti-Flag released their first album, Die For the Government, on New Red Archives.

 

The band began to take off with the release of its second album, A New Kind of Army, in 1999 and joined the Warped Tour.

 

In 2001, Anti-Flag teamed with legendary punk producer Mass Giorgini to record the album Underground Network, released on Fat Wreck Chords.

 

Anti-Flag controversially signed to RCA Records and released their major label debut For Blood and Empire in 2006.

 

The band released a total of thirteen albums, the last of which came out months before their disbandment.



Recording venues evolved dramatically. 

A New Kind of Army, the second Anti-Flag record, was recorded in four or five places, any space they could find that had enough room to set microphones up.

 

The band recorded The People or the Gun in a home studio they built themselves.

 

20/20 Vision recording sessions took place at Dog House Studios and MDDN Studio from July to August 2019, with production handled by Matt Good.





In the wake of the September 11th terrorist attacks, Anti-Flag spoke out against the push for war by the United States government. During this time, many music stores pulled Anti-Flag's records from shelves, as their music was labelled as "anti-American."

 

In 2003, Anti-Flag released the album The Terror State on Fat Wreck Chords, which primarily focused on criticizing the Bush Administration's handling of the War on Terrorism.

 

The album was the first to contain the now-commonplace booklet filled with essays from historians and political commentators, most notably Professor Howard Zinn.



One remarkable moment came when 

the album contains a song whose lyrics were originally written by Woody Guthrie entitled "Post-War Breakout." The track's music was arranged by the members of Anti-Flag, as Guthrie had never committed the arrangements to paper.

 

ANTIfest was Anti-Flag's own hosted festival that originally took place on May 3, 2012, at The Forum at the University of Hertfordshire. ANTIfest was the first self-established festival hosted by the band, who wanted to make it an annual event.

 

The band is well known for its politically charged lyrics and activism, focusing on human rights and various left-wing causes.

 

The band is known for its overtly political songs — criticizing everything from leaders, warfare, prison systems, and early on, the very punk scene they belonged in.

 

Anti-Flag suddenly broke up in July 2023 amidst several rape and sexual assault allegations made against Sane. The other members directly condemned him and disbanded.

mojo nixon & skid roper.rawNotes:
Alright cats, let me lay down the Mojo Nixon & Skid Roper story for you. 

Neill Kirby McMillan Jr. was born in Chapel Hill, North Carolina, on August 2, 1957, and he paired with Skid Roper in the early 1980s in San Diego.

 

Skid Roper was born Richard Banke on October 19, 1954, in National City, California.

 They formed their partnership 

in April 1983

, and the name Mojo Nixon itself came from 

a cross-country bicycle trip when Nixon allegedly came up with his stage name (a combination of "voodoo and bad politics") while roaring drunk in New Orleans.





Nixon and Roper released their first album in 1985 on Enigma Records, Mojo Nixon and Skid Roper.

 

The album was recorded at The Bungalow in Coronado, California, except "Rockin' Religion" and "Black Yo' Eye," recorded at Soundtrax in San Diego.

 

The song "Jesus at McDonald's" from that album was the duo's first single.

 

They recorded their second album, the aptly titled Frenzy, in Los Angeles in 1986, which expanded their cult audience thanks to the novelty hit "Stuffin' Martha's Muffin," an X-rated tribute to MTV VJ Martha Quinn.

 

Frenzy was recorded at Hit City West in Los Angeles, mixed at Mad Dog Studios in Venice, California, and mastered at Capitol.

 

Their third album, 1987's Bo-Day-Shus!!, featured the song "Elvis Is Everywhere," a deification of Elvis Presley, which became Nixon's best known song.

 

Mojo Nixon and Skid Roper were also recorded in San Francisco during these early years by producer Sylvia Massy at CD Presents for the Rat Music For Rat People compilation album.





Nixon and Roper lampooned contemporary American culture and social issues in songs such as "I Hate Banks", "Burn Down the Malls", and "The Amazing Bigfoot Diet".

 

With Nixon, Roper was mainly a percussionist playing washboard, tambourine, maracas, and bongos, but also played mandolin, banjo, harmonica and other instruments.

 They emerged from 

the San Diego dive bar scene of the 1980s at venues like the Spring Valley Inn, the Spirit, and Bodie's.



As for specific anecdotes: 

In August of 1984, the two were victors in a local battle of the bands, and with the studio time they won as a prize, they recorded two songs for a compilation album of San Diego bands, which was the first Nixon music committed to vinyl.

 

At the Fillmore Auditorium in San Francisco in 1987 on a bill with Camper Van Beethoven and Ten Foot Faces, right in the middle of their set, Mojo handed Skid his plugged-in guitar saying he had to pee and ran off backstage, leaving Roper alone on the large stage where he started playing "Ripple," the old Grateful Dead tune, to a thousand eager faces.

 

Country singer John Rich bristled at liberal Mojo in an interview saying "You look like a guy I killed once," and Mojo shot back, "You look like a guy I fucked in prison," causing Rich to storm out.

 

Nixon and Roper parted ways late in 1989.

descendents.rawNotes:
The Descendents are an American punk rock band formed in Manhattan Beach, California, in 1977, by guitarist Frank Navetta, bassist Tony Lombardo and drummer Bill Stevenson as a power pop/surf punk band.

 

In 1979, they enlisted Stevenson's school friend Milo Aukerman as a singer, and reappeared as a melodic hardcore punk band, becoming a major player in the hardcore scene developing in Los Angeles at the time.

 

Since 1986, the band's lineup has consisted of vocalist Milo Aukerman, drummer Bill Stevenson, guitarist Stephen Egerton, and bassist Karl Alvarez.



The band's catalog remains vital and varied. 

Adding singer Milo Aukerman, they released the Fat EP in 1981 and their debut album Milo Goes to College in 1982 through New Alliance.

 

Milo Goes to College reconciled the antisocial fury of Southern California peers like Black Flag with diary-entry vulnerability and sing-along hooks.

 

The band took a hiatus during Aukerman's collegiate studies, reconvening in 1985 for I Don't Want to Grow Up with guitarist Ray Cooper replacing Navetta.

 

Doug Carrion had replaced Lombardo by 1986's Enjoy!, but both he and Cooper soon left the band and were replaced by Karl Alvarez and Stephen Egerton, respectively.

 

Both bands signed to Epitaph Records, with the Descendents releasing Everything Sucks in 1996, which became their first album to chart, reaching #132 on the Billboard 200, and was supported by singles and music videos for "I'm the One" and "When I Get Old".



For recording, 

the band worked at Total Access Recording in Redondo Beach, California for Milo Goes to College in June 1982, with Spot, who had also engineered and produced the Fat EP.

 

For I Don't Want to Grow Up, Stevenson, Aukerman, Cooper, and Lombardo recorded in April 1985 at Music Lab studios in Hollywood, Los Angeles, California, with producer and engineer David Tarling.

 

Enjoy! was recorded in March and April 1986 at Radio Tokyo studios in Venice, California.

 

Everything Sucks was recorded in June and July 1996 at The Blasting Room, a studio built and run by Stevenson in Fort Collins, Colorado.





The Descendents sang about topics that touched many young punks' lives like parents, food, girls and being rejected.

 

The album All was themed around a concept invented by Stevenson and friend Pat McCuistion during a fishing trip on Stevenson's boat Orca in 1980—the concept of "All," meaning doing the utmost, achieving the utmost, turning into their own religion.

 A memorable anecdote: 

The lyrics of "Hürtin' Crüe" derived from a high school classmate of singer Milo Aukerman who had earned a score of 1420 on the SAT, gaining him entry into the United States Military Academy, and the song taunted with the lyrics "I am better than you / You are a piece of poo / 1420".

 

Lead singer Milo Aukerman was a biochemist for the internationally known DuPont Chemical company, based out of Delaware.

 

Bill Stevenson said of the original bandmates: "Frank and Tony, they saved me from being a lonely, miserable little boy, and I'll never forget it."

rancid.rawNotes:
Rancid is an American punk rock band formed in Berkeley, California, in 1991.

 

Founded by Tim Armstrong and Matt Freeman, former members of the band Operation Ivy, Rancid is often credited alongside Green Day and the Offspring as being among the wave of bands that revived mainstream interest in punk rock in the United States during the mid-1990s.

 

Their current lineup consists of Armstrong on guitar and vocals, Freeman on bass and vocals, Lars Frederiksen on guitar and vocals, and Branden Steineckert on drums.

 

The band was formed by Armstrong, Freeman, and former drummer Brett Reed, who left the band in 2006 and was replaced by Steineckert.

 

Frederiksen joined during the subsequent tour after the original trio recorded the band's first album.



The East Bay scene was foundational to Rancid's rise. 

Born from the energy of the East Bay punk scene, Rancid helped carry punk rock from crowded clubs to a global stage while staying deeply rooted in their community-minded, street-level perspective.

 

Rancid's first recorded release was a 1992 EP for Operation Ivy's old label Lookout! Records, then the band was signed to Bad Religion guitarist Brett Gurewitz's record label, Epitaph Records, and released its self-titled debut album through Epitaph in 1993.

 

The band rose to popularity in 1994 with its second studio album, Let's Go, featuring the single "Salvation", and in the following year released its highly successful album ...And Out Come the Wolves, which produced its best-known songs "Roots Radicals", "Ruby Soho", and "Time Bomb", and was certified gold and platinum by the RIAA, selling over one million copies in the United States alone.

 

Its next six albums – Life Won't Wait (1998), Rancid (2000), Indestructible (2003), Let the Dominoes Fall (2009), ...Honor Is All We Know (2014) and Trouble Maker (2017) – were also critically acclaimed, though not as commercially successful as ...And Out Come the Wolves.

 

Rancid entered Skywalker Sound to record a follow-up album in January 2008.



Here's a wild piece of Rancid lore: 

Some of the rumors circulating about the band were that Epitaph employees were not allowed to discuss matters with the press, Rancid convinced an A&R man from Epic to shave a blue mohawk, and Madonna sent the band nude pictures of herself.

 

While Rancid was writing for a follow-up album, Billie Joe Armstrong joined them to co-write the song "Radio", which resulted in Armstrong playing a live performance with Rancid, but after Billie Joe turned down the request to join, Frederiksen changed his mind and joined Rancid.

 Another testament to their hustle: 

By the end of 1998, Rancid had played more than a thousand shows, canceling just one date since the group's formation because of an illness.





Led by Tim Armstrong and Matt Freeman, the band fuses raw punk power with ska and reggae influences, crafting anthems that balance grit, melody, and resilience, while across decades channeling personal struggle, working-class stories, and defiant hope into music that continues to connect across generations.

 

The band has sold over four million records worldwide, making it one of the most successful punk rock groups of all time.

the bouncing souls.rawNotes:
I'll search for information about The Bouncing Souls for you.

The Bouncing Souls are an American punk rock band from New Brunswick, New Jersey, formed in 1989.

 

The core lineup includes Greg Attonito on lead vocals, Pete Steinkopf on guitar, and Bryan Kienlen on bass, all friends who attended high school together in Basking Ridge, New Jersey.

 

The four original members grew up in Basking Ridge and made the decision to move to New Brunswick, a college town home to Rutgers University.

 

The band's name is a derivative of Doc Martens shoes, specifically their advertising slogan "with Bouncing Soles".





Their first full-length album, The Good, The Bad & The Argyle, was released in 1994 on Chunksaah Records, their own label, and was a compilation of several EPs the band had released previously.

 

Maniacal Laughter followed in 1996, with half the album written in a week, and led to a high-profile tour with Youth Brigade that got the attention of Epitaph Records.

 

The Bouncing Souls signed with Epitaph in 1997 and released their self-titled album that year, followed by Hopeless Romantic in 1999.

 

How I Spent My Summer Vacation in 2001 became a fan favorite with songs like "True Believers," "Gone," and "Manthem."

 

The Gold Record came in 2006, produced by Ted Hutt and recorded in Los Angeles.

 

The Argyle EP was recorded at Power Station in Rochester, New York.

 

More recently, they worked with producer Will Yip at Studio 4 for their album Ten Stories High.





By the time of their acknowledgment by the national punk rock scene, they had reignited a "pogo" element to New Jersey punk rock by playing fast light-hearted songs, a model followed by various other local bands.

 

The band organized grassroots shows, founded the independent label Chunksaah Records, and built a tight-knit community of fans known as "True Believers" through relentless touring and direct engagement.



A legendary anecdote involves the song "Gone." 

The song was made when one of the band members was homeless, and he wrote it as he was on a street corner and he heard some kids listening to a song in their parked car when a certain song came on that gave him hope—he wanted to write a song that would give others hope.

 Another tale comes from the Epitaph era: 

the Souls later referred to their self-titled Epitaph debut as a rush job full of songs that didn't feel finished.

 

After Hopeless Romantic's release, personal problems arose between the band and long-time drummer Shal Khichi, resulting in his departure; he was replaced by former Skinnerbox and Murphy's Law drummer Michael McDermott.





Hopeless Romantic found the band exploring more personal lyrical themes while still delivering their energetic punk sound.

 

The Bouncing Souls promoted inclusivity and anti-authority themes through their lyrics and community engagement, critiquing societal control and celebrating personal and collective resilience in songs like "The Beginning of the End," which rails against imposed lies and authority.

 

On their latest full-length, Ten Stories High, the beloved New Jersey punks took fan connection further with an album of songs directly inspired by their fans' very own stories.
